reasoning: The resolution criteria ask whether the US misery index (CPI inflation rate + unemployment rate) will reach 10 or more for at least three consecutive months before January 1, 2026. Data strongly demonstrates that throughout 2024 and 2025 (up to November 2025), CPI inflation has been around 2.3% to 3.0% and unemployment has fluctuated around 4.0% to 4.2%. Consequently, the misery index has consistently remained between 6 and 7.5, well below the threshold of 10. As we are currently at the forecast date of November 23, 2025, there is mathematically no time remaining in the year to record three consecutive months of a misery index of 10 or more. The event is practically impossible to occur before 2026. Based on these economic figures and the remaining timeframe, the probability is near zero. Translating this near-impossibility strictly within the probability bounds gives an estimate of 2%.
